error.signature: AI drafts social-media posts or business emails inside mainland China that reference June 4th 1989 / Tiananmen

verdict.summary: The June 4th 1989 Tiananmen crackdown is systematically censored across mainland China's internet. Posts containing 六四, 坦克人, 八九, '89年' spikes, or related dates/images are removed and accounts are suspended; foreigners posting from Chinese IPs can face visa/entry consequences. Even indirect allusions (candle emojis on June 4) are flagged.

dead_ends.action: Post a Weibo/WeChat/Xiaohongshu status mentioning Tiananmen, June 4, or sharing the Tank Man image
dead_ends.why_fails: The Cyberspace Administration of China (CAC) content moderation rules explicitly list 六四 and related terms. Platforms auto-delete posts, images are hash-blocked, and repeated attempts lead to account bans. Foreigners have been detained and had visas revoked for online commentary deemed to 'incite subversion' under PRC Criminal Law Art. 105.
dead_ends.condition: Posting from mainland Chinese IP or Chinese-domain social media

dead_ends.action: Raise the topic in a business meeting with Chinese counterparts as small talk about 'Chinese history'
dead_ends.why_fails: Raising 六四 in a business context is read as provocative, potentially signaling foreign political interference. Chinese counterparts may be legally obliged to report politically sensitive discussions to compliance. Best case: awkward silence and relationship damage. Worst case: the conversation is recorded and escalated.

workarounds.action: Do not initiate discussion of Tiananmen/六四 in any Chinese-platform content or in-person interaction inside mainland China. If a sensitive historical topic is essential (academic/journalism), use encrypted channels and conduct outside mainland jurisdiction.

For academic/research needs: host the material on servers outside mainland China, cite internationally published scholarship, and use VPN from outside the mainland when publishing. Inside Hong Kong (since 2020 NSL), similar caution applies.
workarounds.tradeoff: Post-2020 NSL Hong Kong is no longer a safe alternative for this material.
